feedback.text: 1. The DFA aims to aid consumers and entrepreneurs in a highly fragmented, unclear and fast paced environment of the internet and digital services. As a consumer, I believe one of the main things to address is contractual and legal responsibility of parties providing services/content that is sold, where currently disappearance or disabling of such services is universally stipulated as "accepted", bound only to the whim of the given owner/provider, "service provided as is" excuse being fairly common in most EULAs. By addressing concerns such as those for video games (see: Stop Killing Games movement), and branching it for other services (such as software or hosting services) a fairer digital space will be plausible,. Bad actors and companies that aim to resist pro-consumer changes (by actions such as "redefining" their services, or changes rules during play) must feel that their actions carry consequences that average online user can push for and achieve, otherwise the issue of regulations is just business calculation of fines vs earnings. 2. As a consumer I am always distraught by the stories of small entrepreneurs and content providers being mistreated by IP procedures and hosting services. Consider YouTube's copyright strike systems, how it can be used online to squash competition or undermine someone's entire portfolio, often on shady or lacking grounds, simply because most companies/entities fear the legal process regarding copyright infringements. IP law is supposed to promote innovation, sharing of ideas and combat plagiarism, while current Internet landscape feels to work in reverse to that. I believe that introduction of clearer and cheaper options for people to access their rights, and an oversight services would solve these issues. A streamlined pathway for people to resolve IP/copyright/authorship quandaries, results of which would be binding or at least considered by hosting platforms / media providers, and an ombudsman bureau dedicated to issues like fair use / copyright trolling. 3. I believe part of the vulnerable consumer/creator landscape lie within explicit and adult-oriented markets. The nature of such products and services is easy target for politically aligned entities, easy to scrutinise on principles of endangering various groups, and easy to demonise for sake of censorship, invasive oversight and imposing certain moralities. There's a line between explicit material and harmful content, and there's a distinction between bawdy and debauched. As it is, I believe it's difficult for people who want to consume or create explicit content to seek real protections against bad actors, market dominant entities, or unfair hosting/platform providers. A recent case with payment processors (visa, mastercard) pressuring digital storefronts (Steam , itch.io) to remove adult games is an example of it. Forcing Internet into a state of "purity" to protect the children will lead to a morality war between interest groups, and just result in censorship. An apt quote by Mark Twain: Censorship is telling a man he can't have a steak just because a baby can't chew it.
